feed.title: Mapping the design space of sibling-aware covenants

entry.title: Mapping the design space of sibling-aware covenants
entry.summary:
The proposals OP_CTV and OP_TEMPLATEHASH (TH) have been pivotal in discussions about their potential to enable sibling-aware covenants in blockchain transactions, similar to those applied in BitVM, Ark, and potentially LN-Symmetry. Unlike OP_CTV, which relies on committing to scriptSigs for implementing such covenants, OP_TEMPLATEHASH offers a simpler approach without necessitating pre-computation. This simplicity is significant as it enhances the feasibility and usability of the implementation in practical applications.
There are several methods proposed to achieve sibling-aware covenants using TH. These include precomputing a hash of all scriptSigs or employing Dynamic MuHash to commit to all indexed prevouts, removing the one at the current index. Other suggestions involve restricting execution to the first input while committing to all other prevouts, or focusing only on the first or next prevout depending on their presence in the transaction sequence. Each method presents its unique trade-offs in terms of complexity and flexibility.
An innovative alternative involves creating a new witness program that indirectly enforces sibling prevout commitment. This includes the Pay-to-Prevout-Anchor (P2PA) method, where an output can only be spent if its program matches the hash of the corresponding prevout. Additional introspection could be achieved through new opcodes like OP_TXHASH, which allows pushing hashes committing to specific prevouts, and OP_CAT, which facilitates introspection via complex scripting.
In the discourse surrounding these methods, Option 5 — committing only to the next prevout — is highlighted as particularly balanced. It aligns closely with the functionality of TH in scenarios with a single input and adequately addresses the needs of Layer 2 protocols that typically involve two inputs. This option does not require additional pre-computation, maintaining simplicity while offering flexibility for more complex multi-input scenarios.
Further information on related topics, including contract-level relative timelocks (CLRT), which are crucial for enhancing capabilities like those in LN-Symmetry, can be explored in detailed discussions and research available at [Delving Bitcoin](https://delvingbitcoin.org/t/contract-level-relative-timelocks-or-lets-talk-about-ancestry-proofs-and-singletons/1353).
